coredata.dc:title: Experimental and theoretical insights on long-term corrosion protection performance of MXene Nanosheets decorated with graphene quantum dots in epoxy coatings
coredata.dc:creator: Ji, Xiaohong
Seif, Abdolvahab
Yuqing, Zhu
Pourhashem, Sepideh
Duan, Jizhou
Rashidi, Alimorad
Hou, Baorong
coredata.dc:description: In this study, MXene nanosheets decorated with zero-dimensional graphene quantum dots and functionalized with 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES-MXene@GQDs) are used as a nanofiller in epoxy coatings. The MXene@GQDs and APTES-MXene@GQDs are characterized by X-ray diffraction, transmission electron microscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The results prove that the GQDs with spherical size (≤ 10 nm) have covered the MXene nanosheets through creating C-O-Ti covalent bonds. The Si-O-C and Ti-O-Si bonds in the APTES-MXene@GQDs indicate that both MXene and GQDs have reacted with the APTES molecules. The results of electrochemical impedance spectroscopy reveal that the low-frequency impedance modulus of epoxy coatings loaded with 0.1 wt% APTES-MXene@GQDs is 4.4 × 109 and 6.4 × 108 Ω.cm2 after 1 day and 7 weeks of immersion in NaCl solution, respectively. Meanwhile, hydrophobic coatings with high adhesion strength are achieved by adding the modified nanofillers. Density Functional Theory calculations emphasize the potential of MXene@GQDs nanohybrids in corrosion protection. The COO covalent bond significantly bolsters stability, making it a strong contender. An efficient electron transfer, as revealed in charge transfer dynamics, reinforces the interactions. The presence of MXene reshapes the electronic landscape, facilitating robust charge transfer processes. Conversely, the systems lacking MXene exhibit less advantageous energy level placement.

XPS data about (a) Survey scan, and high-resolution XPS spectra for (b) Ti 2p, (c) C 1 s, (d) O 1 s, (e) N 1 s, and (f) Si 2p.
The images recorded during water contact angle measurement for the (a) EP, (b) EP/APTES-MXene, (c) EP/APTES-GQDs, and (d) EP/APTES-MXene@GQDs samples.

The (a, b, c, and d) Bode-Phase and (a’, b’, c’, and d’) Nyquist plots for the EP, EP/APTES-MXene, EP/APTES-GQDs, and EP/APTES-MXene@GQDs samples after (a, a’) 1 week, (b, b’) 3 weeks, (c, c’) 5 weeks, and (d, d’) 7 weeks of immersion in the 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. In all these EIS plots, the data related to EP, EP/APTES-MXene, EP/APTES-GQDs, and EP/APTES-MXene@GQDs are shown with black circles, red triangles, blue cubics, and green stars, respectively. SEM results from cross-sections of (a) EP and (b) EP/APTES-MXene@GQDs coatings.

The 2 × 2 supercell view of the MXene@GQDs (COO) system is also shown (c) to show a better interfacial structure (light blue: titanium, red: oxygen, and gray: carbon). The equilibrium distances (Å) are reported. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(Light gray: titanium, dark blue: nitrogen, red: oxygen, yellow: silicon, gray: carbon, and white: hydrogen). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The Fermi energy, EF, corresponds to the zero line

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, MXene nanosheets decorated with zero-dimensional graphene quantum dots and functionalized with 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES-MXene@GQDs) are used as a nanofiller in epoxy coatings. The MXene@GQDs and APTES-MXene@GQDs are characterized by X-ray diffraction, transmission electron microscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The results prove that the GQDs with spherical size (≤ 10 nm) have covered the MXene nanosheets through creating C-O-Ti covalent bonds. The Si-O-C and Ti-O-Si bonds in the APTES-MXene@GQDs indicate that both MXene and GQDs have reacted with the APTES molecules. The results of electrochemical impedance spectroscopy reveal that the low-frequency impedance modulus of epoxy coatings loaded with 0.1 wt% APTES-MXene@GQDs is 4.4 × 109 and 6.4 × 108 Ω.cm2 after 1 day and 7 weeks of immersion in NaCl solution, respectively. Meanwhile, hydrophobic coatings with high adhesion strength are achieved by adding the modified nanofillers. Density Functional Theory calculations emphasize the potential of MXene@GQDs nanohybrids in corrosion protection. The COO covalent bond significantly bolsters stability, making it a strong contender. An efficient electron transfer, as revealed in charge transfer dynamics, reinforces the interactions. The presence of MXene reshapes the electronic landscape, facilitating robust charge transfer processes. Conversely, the systems lacking MXene exhibit less advantageous energy level placement.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: A great deal of economical loss and structural failure in industrial sections is devoted to corrosion phenomenon of metallic structures [1,2]. In this regard, spraying epoxy polymer coatings as a uniform adhesive film on the metals is the most well-known strategy to decrease the side-effects of corrosion [3–5]. The epoxies are categorized as solvent-based and waterborne, and meanwhile, the solvent-based epoxies are widely used (especially as primers) in different industries [6]. The epoxy film acts as a physical barrier against the penetration of corrosive agents toward the metallic structure. Researchers have widely considered various approaches to improve the barrier performance, decrease the defects, and increase the chemical stability of the polymer coatings [5,7,8]. Accordingly, nanofillers with layered impermeable structure are uniformly added into the polymer matrix to achieve epoxy coatings with optimized characteristics. The efficiency of the layered nanofillers in improving the barrier effect of the coatings and increasing the permeation path of the corrosive ions into the coating structure depends on their dispersion quality, their functional moieties, and their inherent characteristics [7,9,10]. Examples of two-dimensional nanofillers with layered morphology are clays [11,12], graphite [13,14], graphene [15,16], graphene oxide [17,18], graphitic carbon nitride [19,20], hexagonal boron nitride [21,22], and MXene (as a new generation of nanomaterials) [23,24] which have been considered for promoting the corrosion resistance and mechanical strength of the epoxies.

To figure out which sheet-like nanofiller is a better candidate to promote the barrier effect of the epoxy coatings, Pourhashem et al. [17,25] compared the epoxy coatings containing graphene and graphene oxide nanosheets. Their results revealed that the dispersion of graphene nanosheets free from functional groups in the epoxy matrix is challenging, and a maximum 0.05 wt% nanofiller could be dispersed uniformly within the coating structure. Meanwhile, the graphene oxide with multi-hydroxyl groups can be dispersed uniformly in the epoxy coating up to 0.1 wt%. The results of electrochemical impedance spectroscopy showed that the impedance modulus values at 0.01 Hz frequency for the neat epoxy, epoxy coating loaded with 0.05 wt% graphene, and epoxy coating loaded with 0.1 wt% graphene oxide after 14 days of immersion in the 3.5 wt% NaCl solution are 2.1 × 104, 31.9 × 106, and 8.5 × 106 Ω.cm2, respectively. Therefore, the homogeneously dispersed graphene nanosheets provided superior anti-corrosion and barrier effects. However, since graphene nanosheets are free from functional moieties, it is difficult to create a chemical bond between them and the polymer matrix; therefore, the graphene oxide nanosheets are preferred, and various approaches including chemical functionalization [26] and/or hybridizing with other nanomaterials [27] are considered to improve their barrier effect. To obtain a stable epoxy coating with long-term corrosion protection, Pourhashem et al. [28–30] considered the role of silane functionalization and nano-silica decoration on the barrier performance of graphene oxide nanosheets, and the impedance modulus at 0.01 Hz frequency of >108 and 107 Ω.cm2 are obtained after 14 days of immersion, respectively; indicating the significant effect of surface treatment on distribution quality, chemical bonds, and barrier resistance of the nanofillers. Also, Li et al. [31] decorated the graphene oxide surface with Fe2O3 and utilized 2-aminoterephthalic acid as a binding agent between the graphene oxide and the Fe2O3 to increase the corrosion resistance of the epoxy coatings. As another example, Zhou et al. [32] utilized the merits of Benzotriazole modified ZIF-8 on melamine phytate nanosheets coupled with polytannic acid-modified graphene oxide for imposing the triple features of self-healing, corrosion resistance, and antibacterial properties in one epoxy coating.

Furthermore, by introducing Ti3C2Tx MXene nanosheets as novel two-dimensional nanomaterials with unique barrier properties, researchers have revealed the positive effect of MXene for providing superior barrier effects in the polymer coatings [5,23,33,34]. The MXene nanosheets, as two-dimensional transition metal carbides and nitrides, possess unique morphological, structural, and chemical inherent characteristics [35–37]. The results obtained by Zhou et al. [38] indicated that the values of impedance modulus at 0.01 Hz frequency for epoxy coatings loaded with 0.1 wt% graphene oxide and MXene after 30 days of immersion in the NaCl solution are 4.46 × 108 and 5.5 × 108 Ω.cm2, respectively. The MXene nanosheets with potential of dissolving in different solvents could create strong interface interactions and stable chemical bonds with the polymer matrices [39,40].

On the other hand, researchers have tried different methods to optimize the properties of the epoxy coatings loaded with MXene nanosheets and achieve higher barrier and corrosion resistance. One method is based on creating functional moieties such as silane [33], co-agents of silane moieties and cerium cations as inhibitors [5], L-Cysteine [41], and polyaniline [42] on the surface of the MXene nanosheets. The other approach is the synthesis of MXene nanohybrids and in this regard, the hybrids of MXene with graphene oxide [34], layered double hydroxides [43], and imine-based covalent organic frameworks [44] are introduced. The third approach is co-modification of MXene nanosheets with functional groups and hybridizing with other nanomaterials, and the examples are MXene hybridized with graphene oxide and functionalized with silane coupling agent [38], MXene hybridized with zirconium phosphate and functionalized with polydopamine [45], and MXene hybridized with silica and functionalized with tannic acid [46]. In this regard, He et al. [47] used the nanohybrid of black phosphate coupled with tannic acid functionalized MXene to improve the corrosion resistance, flame retardancy, and tribological properties of waterborne epoxy coatings. Wu et al. [48] used Poly (dimethyl diallyl ammonium chloride) (PDDA) for modification of MXene nanosheets and inhibiting its aggregation tendency, while Fe3O4 is anchored on the surface of the PDDA-modified MXene. The hybrid of MXene@PDDA-Fe3O4 is developed to accelerate the formation of phosphate coating and enhance the corrosion resistance of the composite coatings.

Accordingly, in this research, the hybrid of MXene nanosheets with zero-dimensional graphene quantum dots (GQDs) (abbreviated as MXene@GQDs) and functionalized with amino-silane moieties are designed for enhancing the barrier effect of the epoxy coating against diffusion of the NaCl solution. The characteristics of the GQDs, MXene, and MXene@GQDs in the epoxy coatings are evaluated and compared. The GQDs are selected for decorating the surface of the MXene nanosheets because the GQDs are composed of a few layers of graphene with multi-functional groups of hydroxyl, carbonyl, and epoxy on the edges of the sp2 carbon [49,50]. In our pursuit of unraveling the electronic underpinnings of corrosion protection mechanisms, Density Functional Theory (DFT) stands as the bedrock of our theoretical exploration. The DFT empowers us to conduct an exhaustive examination of MXene nanosheets bedecked with GQDs in the epoxy coatings.

To synthesize the hybrid of MXene@GQDs, MXene nanosheets dissolved in DI water with a concentration of 10 mg/mL are purchased from Shandong Xiyan New Material Technology Co., Ltd., China. The GQDs are synthesized by a bottom-up method using urea and citric acid under a hydrothermal method, and the details about the synthesis and characterization results are presented in our previously published papers [50,51]. In this regard, 10 mL MXene solution is added into 60 mL DI water, followed by adding 0.1 g GQDs and stirring at room temperature for 30 min. The mixing weight ratio of MXene and GQDs is 1:1. Then, the mixture is transferred into an autoclave with a container made of Teflon, and treated in an oven at a temperature of 100 °C for 4 h. The product of the hydrothermal reaction is centrifuged and washed multiple times with DI water and absolute ethanol (Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company, Shanghai, China). Then, the final product is freeze-dried at −50 °C for 24 h to achieve the MXene@GQDs powders.

Furthermore, to treat the surface of the nanomaterials including MXene, GQDs, and MXene@GQDs with a silane coupling agent, a simple chemical reaction is utilized to achieve APTES-MXene, APTES-GQDs, and APTES-MXene@GQDs, respectively. Accordingly, 2 mL APTES (purity 98%, Aladdin Industrial Corporation, China) is added into 100 mL absolute ethanol, followed by magnetic stirring at room temperature for 30 min. In another beaker, 0.2 g of the required nanomaterial is dissolved in 20 mL DI water by magnetic stirring at room temperature for 30 min. Then, the solution containing the nanomaterials is added into the APTES-ethanol solution and stirred at 80 °C for 8 h. The product is washed with DI water and absolute ethanol by using a centrifuge with a speed of 8000 rpm. Then, one part of the achieved modified nanomaterial is freeze-dried at −50 °C for 24 h for characterization analysis, while the other part of the nanomaterials is dissolved directly in the absolute ethanol under vigorous magnetic stirring to have a well-dispersed nanomaterial in ethanol and the concentration is measured for three times.

To study the effect of the nanofiller on the corrosion resistance of the polymer coatings, solvent-borne epoxy (E-44, Zhenjiang Danbao Resin Co., Ltd.) coatings loaded with 0.1 wt% silane modified nanomaterials including APTES-MXene, APTES-GQDs, and APTES-MXene@GQDs are prepared, and these coatings are abbreviated as EP/APTES-MXene, EP/APTES-GQDs, and EP/APTES-MXene@GQDs, respectively. For this purpose, 3 mL APTES, 10 g hydroxyl terminated-polydimethylsiloxane (h-PDMS, Aladdin Industrial Corporation), and 2 mL dibutyltin dilaurate (DBTD, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company, China) are added sequentially with time intervals of 20 min into 90 g epoxy resin while stirring and heating at 55 ± 5 °C for 20 min. After adding the mentioned nanomaterials into the epoxy resin, the heater is turned off while the mixture is stirring. When the temperature of the mixture reaches room temperature, the required amounts of the nanomaterials dispersed in absolute ethanol (as described in Section 2.1) are poured into the epoxy mixture and stirred for 30 min. Afterward, 45 g curing agent (amine value = 220 ± 20 KOH/g, Zhenjiang Danbao Resin Co., Ltd.) is added to the epoxy mixture, followed by adding 20 mL thinner (Shandong Qilu Chemical Industry, China) to decrease the viscosity of the mixture and prepare it for paint brushing on the pre-cleaned Q235 steel plates. The curing step of the coatings on the steel plates is achieved by keeping the samples for 1 day at room temperature and 3 days in an oven at 60 °C. As a reference sample, pure epoxy coatings (PE) are prepared as mentioned above, only the nanomaterials are not included in the coating composition.

Different techniques are used to characterize the inherent features of the prepared nanomaterials, while the prepared coatings with a thickness of 200 ± 50 μm (measured by Guangzhou Dongru Electronic Technology Co., Ltd.) are characterized to study their corrosion resistance, adhesion strength, and water contact angle. All these methods are described in detail in Table 1 .

To elucidate the intricate mechanisms within our MXene@GQDs nanohybrids, we rely on a robust theoretical foundation and a meticulously crafted computational approach. Our DFT calculations are performed using state-of-the-art electronic structure methods, enabling us to explore the electronic and energetic properties of the nanoscale system with exceptional accuracy. Within the framework of DFT, a well-established exchange-correlation functional of Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) is employed to describe the interactions between electrons and nuclei [52]. The computational parameters include Grimme's method (DFTD) for van der Waals (vdW) corrections [53], allowing to account for dispersion forces that play a critical role in the stability of these nanohybrids. We utilize the double numerical atomic orbitals augmented by a d-polarization (DNP) basis set, which is meticulously optimized for our system to ensure reliability in our results. Additionally, to understand the charge distribution within the nanohybrids, we employ the Hirshfeld method as our charge analysis mechanism. This method provides valuable insights into the redistribution of charge and the electrostatic interactions between the constituent components. Moreover, the COSMO (Conductor-like Screening Model) solvent model is employed to simulate the surrounding environment's influence and to consider the solvation effects on the electronic structure and energetics of the system [54]. This comprehensive computational strategy empowers us to dissect the complex interplay between MXene, GQDs, and APTES molecules while considering critical factors such as van der Waals forces, charge distribution, and solvent effects. It provides a solid foundation for our subsequent analyses and insights, facilitating a deeper understanding of the behavior of these nanohybrids in various environments including those relevant to corrosion protection.

Fig. 1(a) depicts the XRD results of the GQDs, MXene, and MXene@GQDs. The GQDs have a broad spectrum at 2 theta of about 26–30°, which is ascribed to the crystalline structure of the graphite-based materials [55]. The MXene shows a sharp diffraction peak of (002) Ti3C2 at 2 theta of 5.6° [56]. The XRD pattern of MXene@GQDs reveals peaks around 2 theta of 5.6 and 27.2°; the first peak is assigned to the (002) diffraction plane of MXene, and the second one is due to the (002) diffraction plane of the GQDs [57–59]. The XRD results prove that the hybrid of MXene with GQDs is achieved successfully. Further, the FT-IR analysis is used to study the chemical bonds in the MXene@GQDs hybrid before and after modification with the APTES, and the results are presented in Fig. 1(b). The peaks related to –OH (at 3429 cm−1), -OH/-NH (at 3140 cm−1), C=C/C=N (at 1583 cm−1), C=O/C-H (at 1399 cm−1), TiC (at 782 cm−1), and TiO (at 551 cm−1) are discovered in the FTIR spectrum of the MXene@GQDs; indicating the presence of chemical bonds related to both MXene and GQDs in the structure of the nanohybrid. After modifying the surface of the MXene@GQDs with the APTES, the chemical bonds of –OH (at 3453 cm−1), CC (at 1883 cm−1), C=C/C=N (at 1569 cm−1), CO (at 1115 cm−1), Si-O-Si (at 1033 cm−1), TiC (at 771 cm−1), TiO (at 563 cm−1), and SiO (at 468 cm−1) are detected; indicating that the silane agent is grafted on the surface of the nanohybrid [33,38,49,50]. Furthermore, the morphologies of the MXene@GQDs and APTES-MXene@GQDs are considered by TEM, as represented in Figs. 1(c) and 1(d), respectively. The GQDs, which appear as sphere-like nanomaterials with particle sizes smaller than 10 nm, have covered the MXene surface in the case of the MXene@GQDs. However, the sheets are darkly covered after modifying the surface with the APTES. The GQDs with their small particle size could not be observed due to the dense coverage of the APTES molecules on the nanohybrid surface.

Besides, the elemental composition and chemical bonds in the MXene@GQDs and APTES-MXene@GQDs are considered with the help of XPS analysis. The obtained XPS results are shown in Fig. 2 , while the deconvoluted peaks for high-resolution XPS spectra are listed in Table 2 . The main elements of the MXene@GQDs are Ti, C, N, and O. The main constituent elements of the APTES-MXene@GQDs are Ti, C, N, O, and Si; the presence of Si is related to the modification of the hybrid with the APTES. The XPS results, in comparison to the XPS results of MXene presented in our previously published paper [33], clearly show that the chemical bonding between the GQDs and the MXene nanosheets has occurred and appeared as differences between the deconvoluted peaks of the hybrid and the pure MXene. The bonds of TiC both related to the TiC bonds within the MXene structure and TiC bonds between MXene and GQDs are observed, while new peaks of TiN related to the reactions between the functional groups of the GQDs and the MXene are observed. The deconvoluted peaks of high-resolution C 1 s spectrum show the CC graphitic bonds within the GQDs structure. Besides, the high-resolution O 1 s spectrum of MXene@GQDs reveals that the MXene and the GQDs are bonded together through their functional groups (especially hydroxyl groups), and the covalent bonds of C-O-Ti are created between them [60]. After silane functionalization, various peaks related to APTES coverage on the hybrid surface have appeared. Meanwhile, the TiO2 deconvoluted peak has disappeared in the Ti 2p high-resolution spectra, and instead, a new peak imputed to the Ti-O-N is observed; which can be concluded that the TiO2 particles could react with the amine end groups of the APTES. The Si-O-C and Ti-O-Si bonds in the C 1 s and O 1 s spectra of the APTES-MXene@GQDs indicate that the MXene and GQDs have reacted with the molecules of the APTES. Since there are amine groups in the APTES structure, the deconvoluted peaks of the N 1 s spectrum have shifted toward lower binding energies in comparison to the MXene@GQDs sample. Besides, the Si high-resolution spectrum of the APTES-MXene@GQDs is deconvoluted to Si-O/Si-O-C bonds, approving that the silane agent has successfully reacted with the hybrid surface.

The prepared coatings are considered from different aspects to compare their ability against the progress of the corrosion reactions. In the first step of the corrosion phenomenon, the droplets of the electrolyte are in contact with the surface of the coated steel substrate. Hence, the surface properties of the coatings are a controlling factor, which can be evaluated by the water contact angle test. The results reveal that the average water contact angle on the EP, EP/APTES-MXene, EP/APTES-GQDs, and EP/APTES-MXene@GQDs is 75.8, 115.8, 122.7, and 106.1°, respectively. Fig. 3 shows the results of water contact angle measurement. Accordingly, even low amounts of the APTES-modified nanofillers can change the surface property from hydrophilic to hydrophobic. Since the amine end-groups on the APTES-modified nanofillers could chemically react with the epoxy groups in the polymer matrix of the coating, the nanofillers are bonded to the epoxy matrix through these silane agents (as bridging) and they could alter the surface properties of the polymer coating toward repelling the water droplets.

The other point about the coatings is their adhesion strength to the metallic substrate, which is important to protect them against detaching under mechanical forces and to preserve the attachment of the coating to the metallic substrate under corrosion phenomenon. The results of bond strength for the EP, EP/APTES-MXene, EP/APTES-GQDs, and EP/APTES-MXene@GQDs samples are 3.2, 6.0, 3.5, and 6.1 MPa, respectively. It seems that the presence of the MXene and APTES are two controlling factors for altering the bonding strength. The adhesion of the EP coating to the steel substrate is through attractive forces between the hydroxyl groups of the polymer coating and the oxides/hydroxyls on the steel surface. When the APTES-modified MXene nanosheets are added to the epoxy matrix, the Si-O-Fe bonds are also created at the coating/steel interface. These strong chemical bonds are created through the interaction between the active functional groups of the APTES and the hydroxyl groups of the steel substrate. The morphology of the nanofiller (i.e. GQDs with zero-dimensional morphology, MXene with two-dimensional morphology, and MXene@GQDs with both two- and zero-dimensional morphology) also contributes to the adhesion, and the presence of the MXene has a positive effect in increasing the adhesion strength.

The third parameter involved in the corrosion protection performance of the polymer coatings is considered through their barrier behavior. With the main goal to study this property, the EIS is used and the results of Bode, Phase, and Nyquist are presented in Fig. 4 for all the prepared coatings during the time of immersion in the 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. The Bode plots, which are the changes of the impedance modulus (│Z│) as a function of frequency, indicate that the EP sample has the lowest │Z│ values during the immersion time and the barrier performance of the EP sample is completely lost after 3 weeks of immersion. The EP sample shows a resistive behavior due to the penetration of ions and water into the coating structure, and no capacitive region is determined for the EP sample. Meanwhile, the nanocomposites exhibit advanced barrier protection even after 7 weeks of immersion. These samples show both resistive and capacitive regions, and more capacitive behavior is observed for these samples during the first weeks of immersion. The capacitive feature of the nanocomposites beneath alternative voltage indicates that the coating acts as a non-conductive barrier between the corrosive environment and the metallic substrate. By prolonging the immersion time, the resistive behavior is more vivid in the nanocomposite samples; indicating that the penetration of corrosive agents in the nanocomposite samples and the deviation from the capacitive behavior have occurred.

In this regard, the changes of the impedance modulus at 0.01 Hz frequency (│Z│0.01Hz) for the prepared samples during the time of immersion are depicted in Fig. 5 . Accordingly, a significant reduction in the │Z│0.01Hz value of the EP sample is observed, and this value sharply decreases from about 108 Ω.cm2 to 107 and 36,000 Ω.cm2 after 1 and 3 weeks of immersion in the aggressive electrolyte, respectively. Meanwhile, the nanocomposites show more stable corrosion resistance against the NaCl electrolyte, and the │Z│0.01Hz values have decreased with a slower trend. In the case of the EP/APTES-GQDs, the │Z│0.01Hz value is 1.2 × 109 Ω.cm2 in the 1 st day of immersion, which decreases to 8.3 × 106 Ω.cm2 after 7 weeks of immersion in the 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. The │Z│0.01Hz value of the EP/APTES-MXene is 5.3 × 108 and 4.0 × 107 Ω.cm2 after immersion in the NaCl electrolyte for 1 day and 7 weeks, respectively. Amongst all the prepared coatings, the EP/APTES-MXene@GQDs shows the highest │Z│0.01Hz values during the immersion time; i.e. its │Z│0.01Hz value after 1 day of immersion is 4.4 × 109, decreasing to 1.4 × 109, 1.9 × 109, 7.4 × 108, and 6.4 × 108 Ω.cm2 after 1, 3, 5, and 7 weeks of immersion, respectively.

Hence, the EP/APTES-MXene@GQDs sample shows the highest │Z│ values during the corrosion tests. The capacitive region of this sample is remarkably more extensive than the other prepared coatings, indicating that lower amounts of corrosive ions have penetrated into the structure of this coating. Moreover, the EP/APTES-MXene has almost higher │Z│ values than the EP/APTES-GQDs nanocomposite coating during 7 weeks of immersion in the NaCl solution. It seems that the MXene nanosheets have a better barrier than the GQDs, attributed to the sheet-like morphology of the MXene in comparison to zero-dimensional GQDs. The enhanced barrier effect of the coatings follows the order of EP < < EP/APTES-GQDs < EP/APTES-MXene < EP/APTES-MXene@GQDs.

These observations are further proved by data achieved from the Phase plots. The phase angle at high frequency of 105 Hz is a sign of coating adhesion to the metallic substrate and its corrosion protection performance. These plots reveal that the EP sample has the lowest phase angles at high frequencies compared to the nanocomposite coatings. Although the phase angle at high frequency for the EP sample changes remarkably during immersion time, the phase angles at high frequency for the nanocomposite samples are almost steady during the whole time of immersion. The EP/APTES-GQDs, EP/APTES-MXene, and EP/APTES-MXene@GQDs samples show the phase angle of about 85, 87, and 88°, respectively. All these nanocomposite samples have a phase angle at high frequency in the range of 85–90°, indicating their high adhesion to the substrate even after prolonging the immersion time. Therefore, the corrosion products have not been created to delaminate the interface of the polymer coating with the metallic substrate. However, in the case of the EP sample, the phase angle at high frequency decreases significantly after 1 week of immersion; which is due to the creation of corrosion products and decreasing the coating adhesion strength to the metallic substrate.

The Nyquist plots confirm the above-mentioned results; i.e. the nanocomposite samples have a larger radius than the EP one, and meanwhile, the EP/APTES-MXene@GQDs sample has the largest radius than the EP/APTES-GQDs and EP/APTES-MXene. The diameters of the Nyquist plots have reduced after a longer immersion time, and the reduction in the diameter of the coating loaded with the APTES-MXene@GQDs is remarkably controlled.

Furthermore, the EIS experimental results are fitted with the help of ZSimpWin software, and two equivalent circuits including Rs(Qcoat(Rpore(QdlRct))) and Rs(Qcoat(Rpore(Qdl(Rct(QdiffRdiff))))) are used to fit the data with the least chi-square value. The related equivalent circuits are shown in Fig. 6 . The resistance of the NaCl solution is shown with the parameter of Rs, which is related to the presence of electrolyte between the working and reference electrodes in the electrochemical cell. The resistance of the coating pores, charge transfer, and diffusion-controlled phenomenon are abbreviated with Rpore, Rct, and Rdiff parameters, respectively. The constant phase elements related to the coating structure, double layer, and diffusion-controlled phenomenon are regarded as Qcoat, Qdl, and Qdiff, respectively. In these electrical circuits, the constant phase element is used instead of the capacitor, due to deviation of the coating or double layer from highly capacitive performance during penetration of electrolyte into their structure. The constant phase element can be considered a capacitive element independent of frequency with a negative phase between voltages and currents. As a result, the constant phase element interpolates between a resistor and a capacitor. The Qcoat indicates the coating capacitance for up taking water during immersion in the electrolyte. The Qdl is related to the coating delamination and penetration of water to the coating/metal interphase. The Rpore points out the coating porosity and the Rct indicates the substrate protection. Meanwhile, the Rpore and Rct are two important parameters revealing the barrier effect of the coating system toward the corrosion reactions.

Hence, the changes in the Rpore and Rct values during the immersion time are shown in Fig. 7 . These values indicate that different nanofillers would provide different corrosion resistance, and all the prepared nanocomposite samples have significantly higher Rpore and Rct values than the EP sample. The Rpore and Rct values for the EP sample rapidly decrease to the amounts of <106 Ω.cm2 after 3 weeks of immersion, and the barrier effect of this sample is rapidly lost. The APTES-GQDs are not as efficient as APTES-MXene in enhancing the Rpore and Rct values. However, when these two nanomaterials are coupled together and the silane functionalized nanohybrid of APTES-MXene@GQDs is synthesized, the barrier effect is optimized which is higher than either of APTES-GQDs and APTES-MXene.

Besides, to consider the dispersion quality of APTES-MXene@GQDs in the coating matrix, the EP and EP/APTES-MXene@GQDs coated samples are broken under liquid nitrogen, followed by monitoring the cross-section of the samples by SEM analysis. The SEM images in Fig. 8 indicate that the EP sample has a porous structure, and the cross-section of the EP/APTES-MXene@GQDs is compact without any detectable pores. Besides, the nanohybrids are not distinguished within the coating structure, indicating that the APTES-MXene@GQDs nanofiller is completely embedded in the epoxy matrix of the coating.

Here, our focus of primary DFT calculations revolves around elucidating the intricate electronic and energetic facets, casting light upon stability, electron transfer dynamics, and corrosion resistance while considering the diffusion pathways of corrosive agents [67,68]. Through rigorous computational scrutiny, we unlock a trove of invaluable insights, effectively charting a course for the optimized utilization of these nanomaterials in the realm of corrosion protection and beyond.

In the initial phase of our investigation, we embark on a meticulous comparative analysis, aiming to comprehensively fathom the influence of the COO functional group-mediated covalent bond. This analysis draws a sharp contrast between the MXene@GQDs (COO) system and its counterpart, the MXene@GQDs system bereft of this covalent connection (Fig. 9 ). Within the MXene@GQDs (COO) system, the presence of the COO bond prominently elevates stability, vividly manifested by calculated adsorption energy of −483 kJ/mol (non-vdW-corrected Ead = −71 kJ/mol). This covalent linkage, as previously demonstrated in our report [38], can promote the system's resistance to corrosive agents, positioning it as a front-runner for applications in corrosion protection.

Conversely, when we draw parallels between the MXene@GQDs (COO) system and intact graphene adsorption over MXene, a striking dissimilarity comes to the fore. The adsorption energy within this context registers a markedly lower value of −201 kJ/mol (non-vdW = +294 kJ/mol). This substantial discrepancy underscores the exceptional robustness of the MXene@GQDs (COO) interface, a hallmark of covalent bonding. In contrast, the absence of this covalent bond in the intact graphene adsorption scenario may lead to diminished stability and weaker interlayer interactions, thereby rendering it less suitable for corrosion protection applications. Our comprehensive comparative analysis unequivocally underscores the pivotal role of the COO-mediated covalent bond in enhancing stability and fostering effective interactions within the realm of MXene@GQDs nanohybrids. It accentuates the significance of this bond as a reprehensive functional group in the domain of interface engineering for corrosion protection and related applications.

Furthermore, our exploration delves deeper into electronic structure and charge transfer dynamics within these nanohybrids. Employing Hirschfeld analysis, we painstakingly scrutinize the distribution of electron density, revealing a heightened charge transfer of approximately 0.1 |e| from MXene to the graphene sheet, primarily attributable to the COO covalent bond. Notably, the charge transfer from MXene to the graphene sheet amounts to 0.8 |e| and 0.7 |e| for MXene@GQDs (COO) and the MXene@GQDs systems, respectively. This augmented charge transfer mechanism assumes critical significance for electronic conductivity and is instrumental in fortifying the stability of the MXene@GQDs (COO) system. Notably, this observed charge transfer is approximately two times greater than that reported for the MXene@ZnO hybrid system [69], suggesting a higher degree of conductivity in the MXene-GQD system compared to the MXene@ZnO hybrid. This finding clearly underscores the significance of the MXene-GQD hybrid system in potential applications requiring enhanced conductivity. Consequently, stronger interactions ensue, potentially enhancing the efficacy of APTES wrapping on the surface. These computational insights, in tandem with empirical data, notably from XPS analysis, serve as a robust foundation for comprehending the heightened stability and enhanced interactions intrinsic to the MXene@GQDs (COO) system. They offer invaluable implications for a spectrum of applications, ranging from corrosion protection to broader domains of inquiry. Moreover, the unique COO-mediated connection amplifies the stability of APTES over MXene@GQDs (COO) by approximately 6 kJ/mol compared to the MXene@GQDs system, primarily due to intensified electron transfer from MXene to graphene within the MXene@GQDs (COO) system (Fig. 10 ). The heightened charge transfer assumes paramount significance in the interaction of the nanohybrid system with APTES. Notably, these energy values surpass those observed in APTES adsorption over either graphene or MXene, underscoring the pivotal role of MXene as a support, in line with our previous report [38].

Additionally, the covalent connection engenders increased stability between the two layers at the interface, a pivotal element for nanohybrid efficiency and the prevention of corrosive agent diffusion. In our current study, we chose to utilize GQDs as a departure from graphene oxide [38] and harness COO as the covalent connector to assess its significance in interface engineering. The Electron Density Difference (EDD) analysis remains consistent with our previous research, validating the impact of this covalent bond on charge distribution and stability within the MXene@GQDs (COO) system.

In our relentless quest to comprehend the nuanced electronic interactions governing the MXene@GQDs (COO) system, we employ Density of States (DOS) analysis as a powerful investigative tool. This rigorous analysis yields invaluable insights into the electronic structure and the distribution of energy states within our nanohybrid system. Our astute observations unveil a remarkable phenomenon: the presence of MXene as a robust support fundamentally reshapes the electronic landscape within the system.

Specifically, our Projected Density of States (PDOS) analysis, shown in Fig. 11 , provides compelling evidence of a significant shift in the unoccupied orbital levels of APTES to positions residing below the Fermi level. This intriguing shift in energy levels serves as a clear manifestation of the effective orbital hybridization and charge transfer dynamics actively occurring within the MXene/GQDs (COO) system. It vividly illustrates the influential role of MXene as a support, facilitating potent interactions and electronic coupling between the APTES molecules and the GQDs sheet supported by MXene. Consequently, the electronic states that hold paramount importance in APTES adsorption and corrosion protection are strategically positioned for efficient charge transfer processes, amplifying the overall system's efficacy.

In stark contrast, when we draw a comparative analysis with systems devoid of MXene, such as adsorption onto graphene, a distinct dissimilarity unfolds. In the absence of MXene, the unoccupied orbital levels of APTES remain relatively stable, hovering above the Fermi level, as illustrated in Fig. 11. This pronounced disparity in energy level positioning underscores, unequivocally, the pivotal and stabilizing role assumed by MXene as a support within the MXene@GQDs (COO) system.

Our meticulous PDOS analysis serves the dual purpose of unraveling the intricacies of charge transfer and orbital hybridization [70] within the APTES-MXene@GQDs (COO) system and highlighting the indispensable and enhancing contribution of MXene as a support in fortifying the stability and fortitude of corrosion protection mechanisms.

The results show that the corrosion protection behavior of the EP coatings is significantly controlled through barrier effect, although the other two parameters including the wetting properties of the coating surface and the bonds created at the coating/substrate interface have effects on improving the resistance of the coating system against the electrochemical corrosion reactions. When the neat epoxy is sprayed on the substrate, this coating with hydrophilic characteristics, low adhesion strength to the steel substrate, and low resistance against the diffusion of the corrosive agents is not able to provide acceptable corrosion resistance in the NaCl electrolyte. In the case of modifying the epoxy structure by adding the nanofillers, all the features of the epoxy coating have altered which strongly depend on the type of the nanofiller.

The APTES functionalized nanofillers could impose hydrophobicity on the surface of the coating, while strong bonds of Si-O-Metal are created at the interface of the coating with the substrate. Furthermore, the EIS results indicate that the nanofillers improve the barrier performance of the epoxy coating; more capacitive behavior is observed in the case of the nanocomposite samples, and the resistive behavior is limited. Accordingly, the GQDs with zero-dimensional morphology and particle size of <10 nm are not as effective as MXene nanosheets (with sheet-like morphology) in enhancing the barrier performance of the epoxies. Indeed, the EP coatings modified with APTES-GQDs show lower corrosion protection than the EP/APTES-MXene. However, due to the merits of the nanohybrids, the MXene nanosheets decorated with GQDs and functionalized with APTES have shown superior barrier effects. The APTES-MXene@GQDs can improve the stability of the nanocomposite coatings in the corrosive environment through limiting the penetration of the corrosive agents into their structure. In our theoretical analysis by using DFT, we examine how nanofillers influence the barrier effect and stability of nanocomposite coatings. Specifically, we explore the role of APTES-GQDs and APTES-MXene nanofillers, emphasizing their impact on surface hydrophobicity and interfacial Si-O-Metal bonds.

In our theoretical analysis using DFT, we have examined how nanofillers influence the barrier effect and stability of nanocomposite coatings. Our calculations provide insights into charge transfer dynamics and electronic structure within the coatings, elucidating the observed capacitive behavior. We demonstrate how APTES-MXene@GQDs nanohybrids, from a theoretical perspective, effectively restrict the penetration of corrosive agents; thus, enhancing the corrosion resistance. This theoretical framework enhances our understanding about the mechanisms underpinning improved corrosion protection in corrosive environments.

Few-layered MXene nanosheets hybridized with sphere-like graphene quantum dots and modified with APTES are synthesized. The APTES-modified MXene@GQDs hybrid is used as a nanofiller in the solvent-borne epoxy coatings. The presence of the APTES-MXene@GQDs in the epoxy matrix has increased the water contact angle from 75.8° to 106.1°, increased the adhesion strength from 3.2 MPa to 6.1 MPa, and increased the barrier effect of the coating. The enhanced barrier effect is proved by significant enhancement in the Bode, Phase, and Nyquist data, and slowly reduction of the impedance values during 7 weeks of immersion in the NaCl solution, indicating higher quality of the nanocomposite coating compared to the neat one. Moreover, our DFT calculations highlight the potential of MXene@GQDs (COO) nanohybrids in corrosion protection. The COO covalent bond significantly enhances stability, making it a promising candidate. The charge transfer dynamics demonstrate efficient electron transfer, strengthening interactions. The MXene support reshapes the electronic landscape, facilitating potent charge transfer processes. In contrast, systems without MXene exhibit less favorable energy level positioning. Our study underscores MXene's critical contribution to the stability and effectiveness of corrosion protection mechanisms. These findings offer valuable insights for a range of applications, from corrosion protection to broader research areas. The COO covalent bond and MXene support hold significant promise for advancing interface engineering and corrosion protection.
